title: Big-O in Practice — What Java Developers Actually Need

sections.heading: Why it matters in interviews
sections.content:
Senior engineers aren't asked to prove O(n log n) for mergesort. They're asked:
• "Why is this endpoint slow?"
• "Will this scale when we have 10x users?"
• "What's the cost of this loop inside a loop?"

The answer always comes back to understanding which operations are O(1), O(log n), O(n), or O(n²) in the code you write daily.

sections.heading: Java Collections — operation costs
Common complexity traps in Spring Boot
Amortized complexity — why ArrayList.add is O(1)
sections.content:
ArrayList doubles its internal array when full. One expensive O(n) copy happens, but the next n adds are O(1). Averaged out = O(1) amortized.

Same principle applies to:
• HashMap rehashing (at load factor 0.75)
• StringBuilder internal buffer expansion
• Kafka producer batch accumulation

**Interview tip:** When asked "Is ArrayList.add O(1) or O(n)?" — the correct answer is "O(1) amortized, O(n) worst case during resize."

title: Collections Deep Dive — Choosing the Right Structure

sections.heading: Decision matrix
sections.content:
**When to use what:**

| Need | Structure | Why |
|------|-----------|-----|
| Fast lookup by key | HashMap | O(1) get/put |
| Sorted iteration | TreeMap | O(log n), natural order |
| Unique elements | HashSet | O(1) contains |
| Insertion order preserved | LinkedHashMap | O(1) + order |
| FIFO processing | ArrayDeque | O(1) add/poll, no sync overhead |
| Thread-safe map | ConcurrentHashMap | Lock striping, no global lock |
| Thread-safe queue | BlockingQueue | Producer-consumer pattern |
| Immutable snapshot | List.of() / Map.of() | No defensive copy needed |

title: Trees in Real Systems — B-Tree, Trie, and Why It Matters

sections.heading: B-Tree — every database index
sections.content:
When you create a PostgreSQL/MySQL index, it's a **B-Tree**:

```java
CREATE INDEX idx_delivery_status ON delivery(status, scheduled_at);

-- What happens internally:
-- B-Tree with branching factor ~100-500
-- Each node = one disk page (8KB)
-- Depth = log₁₀₀(rows) ≈ 3-4 levels for millions of rows
-- → Finding a row = 3-4 disk reads!

-- That's why indexed lookup is O(log n)
-- And full table scan is O(n) — must read ALL pages
```

**Senior connection:**
• Composite index order matters (leftmost prefix rule) because B-Tree is sorted left-to-right
• Range queries (BETWEEN, >, <) only benefit the LAST column in composite index
• That's why you put equality columns first, range/sort columns last
